blocks.text: The big picture: closed-loop power and refrigeration

blocks.title: Plain-English map
blocks.text: These problems look big because every point has a table lookup, but the story is small. A vapor power plant is four boxes in a loop: add heat, extract work, reject heat, pump back up. A refrigerator is also four boxes: absorb heat where it is cold, compress, reject heat where it is warm, throttle back down. The numbers mostly come from enthalpy differences.

steps.label: Draw the loop first
steps.text: Rankine: pump → boiler → turbine → condenser. Refrigeration: compressor → condenser → valve → evaporator.

steps.label: Build a state table
steps.text: For each state, write what you know: pressure, temperature, quality, entropy, or “saturated liquid/vapor.” Then go to the correct table.

steps.label: Find enthalpies
steps.text: Most heat and work terms are $h$ differences. Do not compute before $h_1,h_2,h_3,h_4$ are known.

steps.label: Apply one device at a time
steps.text: Turbine/compressor/pump give work. Boiler/condenser/evaporator give heat. Valve is usually $h_{in}=h_{out}$.

blocks.text:
A **vapor power plant** boils water, runs the high-pressure steam through a **turbine** to make work, condenses it back to liquid, and **pumps** it back up to boiler pressure. The water continuously cycles between liquid and vapor. Reverse the idea — spend work to move heat from cold to hot — and you have a **refrigerator** or **heat pump**.

The whole topic reduces to one habit: **build a state table** ($p$, $T$, $h$, $s$, $x$ for every numbered point) from the tables, then plug enthalpy differences into the 1st law for each device.

Each device is an **open system at steady state** (SSSF). With negligible kinetic and potential energy changes, the 1st law for one device per unit mass is just $\dot Q_{cv}-\dot W_{cv}=\dot m\,(h_e-h_i)$. Turbine and pump are adiabatic ($\dot Q=0$); boiler and condenser do no work ($\dot W=0$). That single equation generates every formula below.

The standard vapor power cycle: **boiler** (heat in at constant $p$), **turbine** (adiabatic expansion producing work), **condenser** (heat out at constant $p$), **pump** (adiabatic compression of liquid). The *ideal* Rankine cycle assumes no friction/pressure drop in boiler and condenser, and isentropic turbine and pump.
The four devices and their 1st-law equations

blocks.caption: Turbine (1→2) and condenser (2→3). State 1 = turbine inlet, 2 = turbine exit, 3 = condenser exit (saturated liquid).
Pump (3→4) and boiler (4→1). Pump consumes work (negative). $v_3$ is the saturated-liquid specific volume at the condenser.

blocks.title: Pump work shortcut
blocks.text: For a liquid, $v$ is almost constant, so the reversible shaft work $-\int v\,dp$ collapses to $\dfrac{\dot W_p}{\dot m}=-v_3\,(p_4-p_3)$. Watch the **units**: $v$ in $\mathrm{m^3/kg}$ times $\Delta p$ in $\mathrm{kPa}$ gives $\mathrm{kJ/kg}$. With $v_3\approx1.008\times10^{-3}$ and $\Delta p=800-8=792\ \mathrm{kPa}$ you get about $0.8\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$ — tiny next to the turbine's hundreds of kJ/kg.

blocks.caption: Thermal efficiency. The pump term is added with its own sign ($W_p$ is negative), or subtract its magnitude ($h_4-h_3$).
Back-work ratio. In a steam cycle it is tiny (~0.1-1%) because pumping a liquid is cheap; in a gas (Brayton) cycle it is large.

blocks.title: Professor's favourite mistakes
blocks.text:
- **Sign of pump work.** $\dot W_p/\dot m=h_3-h_4$ is **negative**. In $\eta$ the *net* work is $W_t+W_p$ (add the negative number) — do not add the magnitude.
- **K vs °C** only matters in Carnot formulas; table look-ups use the actual temperature, but $T_C/(T_H-T_C)$ needs **kelvin**.
- **Quality before enthalpy.** Find $x$ from entropy first, then get $h$ with the same $x$. Don't mix an $s$-based $x$ into an $h$ table for a superheated point.
Wet states and quality

Inside the vapor dome a point is a liquid+vapor mixture. $x=\dfrac{m_{vap}}{m_{liq}+m_{vap}}\in[0,1]$. Any specific property interpolates linearly: $h=(1-x)h_f+x\,h_g$, and identically for $v$, $u$, $s$. To find $x$ at a known $p$ and $s$: $x=\dfrac{s-s_f}{s_g-s_f}$.

blocks.title: Quality at an isentropic turbine exit
blocks.text:
Steam enters a turbine as saturated vapor at $8\ \mathrm{bar}$: $h_1=2768.9$, $s_1=6.6625\ \mathrm{kJ/kgK}$. It expands isentropically to the condenser at $0.08\ \mathrm{bar}$, where $s_f=0.5925$, $s_g=8.2267$, $h_f=173.9$, $h_g=2576.1$.

- $x_{2s}=\dfrac{s_1-s_f}{s_g-s_f}=\dfrac{6.6625-0.5925}{8.2267-0.5925}=0.795$.
- $h_{2s}=(1-0.795)(173.9)+0.795(2576.1)=2083.6\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$.

Low quality (0.795) means ~20% liquid droplets — bad for turbine blades, a key design concern.

blocks.title: Rankine cycle on T-s
blocks.caption: Drag boiler pressure, condenser pressure and superheat. Watch the cycle area (net work), the efficiency, and the exit quality move. Confirm: raising boiler $p$ or superheat raises $\eta$; lowering condenser $p$ raises $\eta$ but can drop the exit quality.

blocks.text: Improving efficiency: pressure, temperature, superheat

A fully reversible cycle between two reservoirs obeys $\eta_{max}=1-T_C/T_H$. So efficiency rises if we **add heat hotter** (higher average $T$ of heat addition) or **reject heat colder**. Three knobs follow directly.

blocks.title: The three levers
blocks.text:
- **Raise boiler pressure** (fixed condenser): higher average heat-addition temperature $\to$ $\eta$ up. But the turbine exit moves left $\to$ **quality drops** (erosion risk).
- **Lower condenser pressure**: lower heat-rejection temperature $\to$ $\eta$ up. Again exit quality drops.
- **Superheat** (heat past saturated vapor into the superheated region): raises average heat-addition temperature $\to$ $\eta$ up, **and** raises exit quality. Best of both — that's why real plants superheat.

Steam expands in a high-pressure turbine to an intermediate pressure, is sent **back to the boiler to be reheated**, then expands in a low-pressure turbine to the condenser. This keeps the expansion line in the high-quality region. Turbine work $=(h_1-h_2)+(h_3-h_4)$ and $\dot Q_{in}=(h_1-h_6)+(h_3-h_2)$, where 2 is HP-exit, 3 reheat-exit, 4 LP-exit, 6 boiler inlet.

A fraction $y$ of steam is **extracted** from the turbine and mixed with the feedwater to preheat it, raising the average heat-addition temperature. The mixing chamber (open FWH) gives, from energy balance, $y=\dfrac{h_6-h_5}{h_2-h_5}$ where 2=extracted steam, 5=pumped condensate, 6=saturated-liquid FWH exit.

blocks.caption: Regenerative cycle efficiency (one open FWH). Only fraction (1-y) flows through the LP turbine and condenser; the full flow goes through the boiler and the second pump.

question.prompt: At fixed boiler pressure you **lower** the condenser pressure of an ideal Rankine cycle. What happens to thermal efficiency and to the steam quality at the turbine exit?
question.explanation: Lower condenser pressure means a lower heat-rejection temperature, so $\eta=1-T_C/T_H$ logic gives **higher** $\eta$. But the turbine now expands to a lower pressure, pushing the exit point left/down under the dome, so **quality drops** (more droplets). B reverses $\eta$; C is wrong on quality (only superheat raises both); D is wrong because $\eta$ does change.
question.theory: Lowering $T_C$ raises efficiency but worsens exit quality; superheat is the lever that improves both.

blocks.text: A reliable Rankine solution method

blocks.title: How to solve a Rankine problem

steps.text: Sketch the T-s cycle and number the four states (1 turbine in, 2 turbine out, 3 condenser out = sat. liquid, 4 pump out).
State 1: from boiler $p$ (and $T$ if superheated) read $h_1$, $s_1$. State 3: from condenser $p$ read $h_3=h_f$, $v_3=v_f$, $s_3=s_f$.
State 2s: set $s_{2s}=s_1$. If wet, $x_{2s}=(s_1-s_f)/(s_g-s_f)$ then $h_{2s}$; if superheated, read $h_{2s}$ from tables. Apply $\eta_t$: $h_2=h_1-\eta_t(h_1-h_{2s})$.
Pump: $\dot W_p/\dot m = -v_3(p_4-p_3)$; $h_4=h_3 - \dot W_p/\dot m$ (so $h_4 = h_3 + v_3\cdot\Delta p$).
If $\dot W_{net}$ given: $\dot m = \dot W_{net} / (w_t + \dot W_p/\dot m)$. Then $\dot Q_{in}=\dot m\cdot q_{in}$, $\dot Q_{out}=\dot m\cdot q_{out}$.

blocks.text: Vapor-compression refrigeration & heat pumps

Reverse the loop and spend work to pump heat uphill. Four devices: **evaporator** (heat in from the cold space, 4→1), **compressor** (work in, 1→2), **condenser** (heat out to warm surroundings, 2→3), and an **expansion valve** (throttle, 3→4). The turbine of the power cycle is replaced by a cheap **throttling valve** because the fluid is low-quality and a turbine would gain little work — and the compressor sucks saturated (or superheated) vapor to avoid liquid droplets.

A flow restriction with $\dot Q=0$, $\dot W=0$ and negligible KE/PE. The 1st law gives $h_{in}=h_{out}$ — an **isenthalpic** process. So across the valve $h_3=h_4$. It produces a simultaneous pressure and temperature drop (Joule-Thomson effect, $\mu_{JT}=(\partial T/\partial p)_h$).

blocks.tex: \frac{\dot Q_{in}}{\dot m}=h_1-h_4,\quad \frac{\dot W_c}{\dot m}=h_1-h_2<0,\quad \frac{\dot Q_{out}}{\dot m}=h_3-h_2,\quad h_3=h_4
blocks.caption: Refrigeration device energies. State 1 = compressor in (sat. vapor), 2 = compressor out, 3 = condenser out (sat. liquid), 4 = after valve.

blocks.tex: \beta=\frac{\dot Q_{in}/\dot m}{|\dot W_c|/\dot m}=\frac{h_1-h_4}{h_2-h_1},\qquad \gamma=\frac{|\dot Q_{out}|}{|\dot W_c|}=\frac{h_2-h_3}{h_2-h_1}=\beta+1
blocks.caption: COP of a refrigerator (β, useful = heat removed) and of a heat pump (γ, useful = heat delivered). γ = β + 1.

blocks.title: Carnot limits
blocks.text: $\beta_{Carnot}=\dfrac{T_C}{T_H-T_C}$ and $\gamma_{Carnot}=\dfrac{T_H}{T_H-T_C}$, with **T in kelvin**. Real cycles fall below: the refrigerant must be colder than $T_C$ in the evaporator and hotter than $T_H$ in the condenser, and the throttling is irreversible.

blocks.title: Compressor isentropic efficiency goes the other way
blocks.text: For a **compressor/pump** $\eta_{is,c}=\dfrac{h_{2s}-h_1}{h_2-h_1}$ (ideal work over actual). So $h_2=h_1+\dfrac{h_{2s}-h_1}{\eta_{is,c}}$ — the real exit enthalpy is **larger**. Don't reuse the turbine formula $\eta_t=(h_1-h_2)/(h_1-h_{2s})$ here; mixing them up is a classic sign error.

blocks.title: Vapor-compression cycle on p-h
blocks.caption: Move evaporator and condenser temperatures and the compressor efficiency. See the throttle as a vertical ($h=$const) line, the COP, and how a bigger temperature lift lowers $\beta$.

question.prompt: In an ideal vapor-compression refrigeration cycle, which process is modeled as isenthalpic ($h$ = constant)?
question.explanation: A throttling valve has $\dot Q=\dot W=0$ and negligible KE/PE, so the 1st law gives $h_{in}=h_{out}$ — isenthalpic. A: compression is (ideally) isentropic, not isenthalpic, and adds enthalpy. B and D are constant-**pressure** heat exchanges where $h$ changes a lot as the fluid condenses/evaporates.
question.theory: Throttling: $\dot Q=\dot W=0 \Rightarrow h_3=h_4$. It replaces the turbine in refrigeration cycles.

practice.prompt: In the ideal Rankine cycle, the pump compresses which state, and why is its work small?

options.content: Superheated vapor; because vapor has low density
Saturated liquid; because the liquid's specific volume is very small
A two-phase mixture; because quality is near zero
Saturated vapor; because entropy is constant

practice.explanation: The pump takes **saturated liquid** from the condenser (state 3) to boiler pressure. Reversible work $-\int v\,dp\approx v(p_4-p_3)$ is small because liquid $v\approx10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^3/kg}$ is tiny. A/D are wrong: pumping vapor would need huge work and is what the compressor does in a gas cycle; the device handling liquid is the pump. C is wrong: at state 3 it is fully saturated liquid ($x=0$), not a mixture.
practice.theory: Pump work $=v_3(p_4-p_3)$; small because liquid specific volume is small — this gives the low back-work ratio of steam cycles.

practice.prompt: Saturated vapor at 8 bar ($h_1=2768.9$, $s_1=6.6625$) expands isentropically to 0.08 bar ($s_f=0.5925$, $s_g=8.2267$). The exit quality $x_{2s}$ is closest to:
practice.explanation: $x_{2s}=(s_1-s_f)/(s_g-s_f)=(6.6625-0.5925)/(8.2267-0.5925)=6.0700/7.6342=0.795\approx0.80$. A, B and D come from arithmetic slips (e.g. forgetting to subtract $s_f$ in the numerator, or using $s_g$ wrongly). Always subtract $s_f$ from both numerator and denominator.
practice.theory: At a known $p$ and $s$ in the wet region, $x=(s-s_f)/(s_g-s_f)$.

practice.prompt: A steam turbine inlet is $h_1=2768.9\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$ and the isentropic exit is $h_{2s}=2083.6\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$. With $\eta_{t}=0.85$, the actual specific turbine work $h_1-h_2$ is:
practice.explanation: Actual work $=\eta_t(h_1-h_{2s})=0.85\times(2768.9-2083.6)=0.85\times685.3=582.5\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$. A is the **ideal** (isentropic) work — forgetting to multiply by $\eta$. C divides by $\eta$ instead of multiplying (that's the compressor mistake). D is $0.25\times685.3$, a random factor. For a turbine, actual < ideal.
practice.theory: $\eta_t=(h_1-h_2)/(h_1-h_{2s})$, so actual work $=\eta_t\times$ ideal work, smaller than ideal.

practice.prompt: For the Tutorial 3.1 cycle, $w_t=582.5$, pump work $=0.8\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$, $q_{in}=2594.2\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$. The thermal efficiency is closest to:
practice.explanation: $\eta=(w_t-w_{pump})/q_{in}=(582.5-0.8)/2594.2=581.7/2594.2=0.224$. A drops the pump term and divides by a slightly different $q$. C and D use wrong $q_{in}$ (e.g. using $h_1$ instead of $h_1-h_4$ or a sign error). The pump term barely matters here but the method must subtract it.
practice.theory: $\eta=(\dot W_t+\dot W_p)/\dot Q_{in}$ with $\dot W_p$ negative.

practice.prompt: Why is the back-work ratio of a Rankine (steam) cycle much smaller than that of a Brayton (gas) cycle?

options.content: Because the steam turbine produces less work
Because pumping a liquid costs far less than compressing a gas
Because the steam condenser rejects more heat
Because steam has a higher specific heat

practice.explanation: $bwr=W_{pump}/W_{turbine}$. In Rankine the working fluid is compressed as a **liquid** (small $v$, tiny $v\Delta p$ work), so bwr ~0.1-1%. In Brayton the compressor squeezes a **gas** (large $v$), so bwr can be 40-60%. A is false (steam turbines produce a lot of work). C and D are irrelevant to the work ratio.
practice.theory: bwr is set by how cheap the compression step is; compressing liquid (pump) is cheap, compressing gas is expensive.

practice.prompt: Adding **superheat** to an ideal Rankine cycle (heating steam into the superheated region before the turbine) does which of the following?

options.content: Raises efficiency and raises turbine-exit quality
Raises efficiency but lowers turbine-exit quality
Lowers efficiency but raises exit quality
Leaves efficiency unchanged but raises exit quality

practice.explanation: Superheat raises the **average temperature of heat addition** ($\eta$ up) and moves the turbine exit to the right, **increasing** exit quality (sometimes still superheated) — fewer droplets. B describes raising boiler pressure or lowering condenser pressure. C contradicts the $\eta$ increase. D is wrong because $\eta$ rises.
practice.theory: Superheat is the lever that improves both $\eta$ and exit quality, unlike pressure changes.

practice.prompt: In a reheat Rankine cycle (states: 1 HP-in, 2 HP-out, 3 reheat-out, 4 LP-out, 5 condenser-out, 6 boiler-in), the heat input rate per unit mass is:
practice.explanation: Heat is added in two places: the main boiler $(h_1-h_6)$ and the reheater $(h_3-h_2)$, so $q_{in}=(h_1-h_6)+(h_3-h_2)$. A forgets the reheat. C is the total **turbine work**, not heat in. D mixes the reheater inlet/outlet wrongly (uses LP-out $h_4$ instead of HP-out $h_2$).
practice.theory: Reheat adds heat twice: boiler and reheater. $q_{in}=(h_1-h_6)+(h_3-h_2)$; $w_t=(h_1-h_2)+(h_3-h_4)$.

practice.prompt: In an open feedwater heater (regeneration), extracted steam ($h_2$) mixes with pumped condensate ($h_5$) to leave as saturated liquid ($h_6$). The extraction fraction $y$ is:
practice.explanation: Energy balance: $y\,h_2+(1-y)h_5=h_6$. Solve: $y(h_2-h_5)=h_6-h_5$, so $y=(h_6-h_5)/(h_2-h_5)$. B uses the wrong numerator difference; C has the wrong denominator; D is the reciprocal of A (it would give a value >1, impossible for a fraction). $y$ must lie between 0 and 1.
practice.theory: Open FWH mass+energy balance: $y h_2+(1-y)h_5=h_6 \Rightarrow y=(h_6-h_5)/(h_2-h_5)$.

practice.prompt: For R134a refrigeration with $h_1=235.31$ (compressor in), $h_4=h_3=106.19$ (after valve), and $h_2=284.1\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$ (compressor out), the COP (refrigeration) is closest to:
practice.explanation: $\beta=(h_1-h_4)/(h_2-h_1)=(235.31-106.19)/(284.1-235.31)=129.12/48.79=2.65\approx2.64$. A is $\gamma=\beta+1$ (heat-pump COP) — the wrong useful effect. C subtracts 1 wrongly. D is the inverse, $|W_c|/Q_{in}$.

practice.prompt: A heat pump and a refrigerator run on the same cycle between the same temperatures. If the refrigeration COP is $\beta = 2.64$, the heat-pump COP $\gamma$ is:
practice.explanation: $\gamma=|Q_{out}|/|W|=(Q_{in}+|W|)/|W|=\beta+1=3.64$. A confuses the two COPs; B subtracts 1 (wrong direction); D doubles $\beta$. The heat pump's useful output (heat delivered) always exceeds the refrigerator's (heat removed) by exactly the work, hence +1.

practice.prompt: For a Carnot refrigerator between $T_C=273\ \mathrm{K}$ and $T_H=299\ \mathrm{K}$, the maximum COP $\beta_{Carnot}$ is closest to:
practice.explanation: $\beta_{Carnot}=T_C/(T_H-T_C)=273/(299-273)=273/26=10.5$. A and C are arithmetic slips on the 26 K span. D uses $T_H/T_C$, which is meaningless here. The real cycle ($\beta=8.9$ in Tutorial 3.4) is below this limit, as required.
practice.theory: $\beta_{Carnot}=T_C/(T_H-T_C)$ with $T$ in kelvin; the actual cycle's COP is always lower.

practice.prompt: A compressor takes R134a from $h_1=235.31$; the isentropic exit is $h_{2s}=276.8\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$ and $\eta_{is,c}=0.85$. The actual exit enthalpy $h_2$ is:
practice.explanation: $\eta_{is,c}=(h_{2s}-h_1)/(h_2-h_1)$, so $h_2=h_1+(h_{2s}-h_1)/\eta=235.31+41.49/0.85=235.31+48.81=284.1$. A multiplies by $\eta$ (turbine logic — wrong for a compressor). C ignores irreversibility. D applies a wrong factor. Real compression overshoots the ideal exit.

practice.prompt: In a Rankine cycle the turbine isentropic efficiency is below 1. Compared with the ideal cycle, the actual turbine-exit state has:
practice.explanation: Irreversibility means less work is extracted, so the actual exit enthalpy $h_2>h_{2s}$ (**higher $h$**), and friction generates entropy, so $s_2>s_{2s}$ (**higher $s$**) — the expansion line tilts right. A is the ideal/reversed picture. C wrongly keeps $h$ fixed. D violates the second law (entropy cannot drop in an adiabatic irreversible process).
practice.theory: Real adiabatic turbine: $h_2>h_{2s}$ and $s_2>s_{2s}$; the cycle does less work and rejects more heat.

practice.prompt: Why is the turbine of a vapor-compression refrigeration cycle replaced by a throttling valve?

options.content: A valve is reversible while a turbine is not
The fluid is low-quality liquid-rich, so a turbine would recover very little work
A valve raises the fluid temperature, which is desired
A turbine cannot operate at high pressure

practice.explanation: At the condenser exit the refrigerant is nearly saturated liquid; expanding it yields almost no work, so a cheap, robust **throttling valve** (isenthalpic) is used instead. A is backwards — the valve is the **irreversible** device. C is wrong: the valve drops $T$ (the goal). D is false; turbines handle high pressure routinely. The trade is simplicity/cost over a negligible work gain.
practice.theory: Refrigeration replaces the turbine with a throttle ($h_3=h_4$) because liquid expansion recovers little work and the valve is cheap and reliable.

practice.prompt: Steam at 70 bar, 500 °C ($h_1=3410.5$) expands to 0.3 bar with $\eta_t=0.85$. Given $h_{2s}=2293\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$, the specific power output $\dot W_{12}/\dot m$ is closest to:
practice.explanation: Ideal drop $=h_1-h_{2s}=3410.5-2293=1117.5$. Actual $=\eta_t\times1117.5=0.85\times1117.5=950\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$. A is the **ideal** work (forgot $\eta$). C uses $\eta^2$ or a wrong factor. D divides by $\eta$ (compressor mistake). For a turbine, multiply the ideal drop by $\eta$.

practice.prompt: Raising the **boiler pressure** of an ideal Rankine cycle at fixed condenser pressure typically:

options.content: Decreases efficiency and increases exit quality
Increases efficiency and decreases exit quality
Increases both efficiency and exit quality

practice.explanation: Higher boiler pressure raises the average heat-addition temperature $\to$ **$\eta$ up**. But the isentropic expansion now ends further left under the dome $\to$ **exit quality down** (droplet/erosion risk), which is why superheat or reheat is added. A reverses $\eta$. C is wrong on quality. D is false.
practice.theory: Higher boiler $p$: $\eta$ up but exit quality down — opposite quality effect to superheat.

exam.title: Simple Rankine cycle: full performance
exam.statement: In a Rankine cycle saturated vapor enters the turbine at 8 bar and saturated liquid leaves the condenser at 0.08 bar. The turbine isentropic efficiency is 85% and the net power output is 100 MW. Determine the thermal efficiency, the back-work ratio, the steam mass flow rate, and the rates of heat in and out.
exam.tips: Convert bar to kPa for the pump term ($\times100$). Keep the pump-work sign negative when forming net work. The condenser duty must equal $\dot Q_{in} - \dot W_{net} = 446.2 - 100 = 346.2\ \mathrm{MW}$ — a fast check.

exam.title: Superheated + reheated Rankine cycle
exam.statement: Steam enters the HP turbine at 80 bar, 480 °C and expands to 10 bar; it is reheated to 440 °C, then expands in the LP turbine to 0.08 bar. $\dot W_{net}=100\ \mathrm{MW}$. Treating both expansions as isentropic, find the thermal efficiency, mass flow rate, and $\dot Q_{in}$, $\dot Q_{out}$.

steps.title: HP exit (2s)
steps.content: At 10 bar with $s=6.6589$ the steam is superheated; from tables $T\approx190\ \mathrm{°C}$, $h_{2s}\approx2803\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$.

exam.finalAnswer: $\eta = 0.42$; $\dot m = 64.5\ \mathrm{kg/s}$; $\dot Q_{in} = 240\ \mathrm{MW}$; $\dot Q_{out} = -140\ \mathrm{MW}$. (With $\eta_t=0.85$ on both stages instead: $\eta'\approx0.365$, $\dot m'\approx75.9\ \mathrm{kg/s}$, $\dot Q_{in}'\approx274\ \mathrm{MW}$.)
exam.tips: Reheat adds heat twice — never forget the $(h_3-h_{2s})$ term in $q_{in}$. Check: $\dot Q_{in}-|\dot Q_{out}| = 240-140 = 100\ \mathrm{MW} = \dot W_{net}$.

exam.title: R134a refrigeration / heat-pump analysis
exam.statement: R134a runs a simple vapor-compression cycle: vaporisation at -20 °C, condensation at 40 °C, $\dot m=10\ \mathrm{kg/s}$, compressor isentropic efficiency 0.85. Find the compressor power, the evaporator and condenser heat rates, and the refrigeration COP. Then state the heat-pump COP.
exam.tips: Compressor efficiency divides (actual work > ideal) — opposite to a turbine. Energy check: $|\dot Q_{out}| - \dot Q_{in} = 1779-1291 = 488 = |\dot W_c|$.

exam.title: Conceptual: draw and explain the ideal Rankine cycle and how to improve it
exam.statement: Sketch the ideal Rankine cycle on a T-s diagram, label the four devices and four states, write the 1st-law energy equation for each device, and explain three ways to raise the thermal efficiency, noting the effect of each on the turbine-exit quality.
exam.given: Working fluid: water. Ideal assumptions: no pressure drop in boiler/condenser; isentropic turbine and pump.

steps.title: Diagram and states
steps.content: On T-s: 4→1 boiler (rightward at high $p$, into/through the dome to sat. vapor), 1→2 turbine (vertical down, isentropic), 2→3 condenser (leftward at low $p$ to sat. liquid), 3→4 pump (vertical up, isentropic). The enclosed area = net work per unit mass.

steps.title: Efficiency expression
steps.content: $\eta=(w_t+w_p)/q_{in}=1-(h_2-h_3)/(h_1-h_4)$. Reversible reference: $\eta_{max}=1-T_C/T_H$, so raise average heat-addition $T$ or lower heat-rejection $T$.

steps.title: Improvement 1 — raise boiler pressure
steps.content: Higher average $T$ of heat addition $\Rightarrow$ $\eta$ up. Exit quality decreases (erosion risk).

steps.title: Improvement 2 — lower condenser pressure
steps.content: Lower heat-rejection $T$ $\Rightarrow$ $\eta$ up. Exit quality also decreases.

steps.title: Improvement 3 — superheat (and reheat/regeneration)
steps.content: Superheating raises average heat-addition $T$ $\Rightarrow$ $\eta$ up AND raises exit quality. Reheat keeps the LP expansion dry; regeneration (open FWH) preheats feedwater with extracted steam, raising the mean heat-addition temperature.

exam.finalAnswer: Ideal Rankine = isentropic turbine & pump + constant-$p$ boiler & condenser; $\eta=1-(h_2-h_3)/(h_1-h_4)$. Efficiency rises by raising boiler pressure, lowering condenser pressure, or superheating; only superheat also improves exit quality. Reheat and regeneration further raise the mean heat-addition temperature.
exam.tips: In a diagram answer, always mark the saturation dome and which segments are isentropic (vertical) vs. constant-pressure. Mention the quality/erosion trade-off — examiners look for it.

title: Vapor Power & Refrigeration Cycles
summary: Vapor cycles are table-based loop problems. Rankine turns heat into work with boiler-turbine-condenser-pump; vapor compression uses compressor-condenser-valve-evaporator to move heat uphill. The core skill is making a state table, finding $h$ at each state, then doing simple device energy balances.
objectives: Draw Rankine and vapor-compression cycles and label the four devices in order.
Create a state table with pressure, temperature/quality/entropy, and enthalpy for each point.
Use turbine, pump, boiler, condenser, compressor, valve, and evaporator energy balances.
Compute thermal efficiency, refrigeration COP, and heat-pump COP.
Check turbine exit quality and understand why wetness matters.
Explain how pressure, superheat, and regeneration/reheat ideas affect performance.
Use steam/R134a tables without losing the physical meaning of the cycle.
